text: I'm sharing a repair of a symptomatic right inguinal hernia in a young man. This has been going on for about six months and he finally chose to come in for a repair.
The surgeon reveals incarcerated omentum that is visibly adherent to the inguinal canal during the hernia repair procedure.
And once I was taken out, I'm just going to do my repair.
The surgeon dissects into the preperitoneal plane, extending from the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) to the medial umbilical ligaments.
The surgeon begins the medial dissection of the right inguinal hernia repair, focusing on separating tissue layers in the medial aspect of the surgical field.
The surgeon adjusts the tissue to elevate the fat and subsequently lowers the bladder, creating a clearer view of the surgical field.
The surgeon performs medial dissection followed by lateral dissection, revealing the sequential steps of the procedure.
The surgeon creates space in the preperitoneal plane for mesh placement, preparing the area for hernia repair.
The surgeon leaves some fat on the abdominal wall while identifying the cord structure, briefly inspecting the area.
The surgeon accesses the cord structures at the level of the anal ring, maneuvering to expose these anatomical features.
The surgeon sweeps the cord structures downward and maneuvers out of the inner-warf metal area, adjusting the position of the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon has cleared the vas deferens and cord structures, leaving the tip of the hernia sac without pursuing it further into the inguinal canal.
The surgeon clears fat over the femoral space to check for the presence of a hernia, then proceeds to dissect down to the obturator space.
The surgeon prioritizes the cord structures, creating space for mesh placement by manipulating tissues inferiorly and laterally.
The surgeon points out the four different hernia locations within the inguinal canal, visually identifying each site for the observers in the room.
The surgeon confirms the absence of a cord lipoma and proceeds to place the surgical mesh over the inguinal canal area.
He was pretty pleased with how that laid.
The surgeon covers the medial pubic bone (MPO) with mesh and proceeds to close the flap using sutures.
The surgeon identifies a small hole in the hernia sac and uses a suture to close the flap, ensuring proper closure of the defect.
And that keeps it closed.
The surgeon uses an angiocath to evacuate gas from the peritoneal flap, preparing the area for further surgical steps.
The case went well. The patient was discharged about an hour later and is doing well. Hope you enjoyed.
